title: Internal Control Module Memory Check Sum Error
shortDescription: The PCM/ECM has detected an internal memory checksum error, indicating corrupted data in the control module's memory.
fullDescription: Code P0601 is set when the Powertrain Control Module (PCM) or Engine Control Module (ECM) performs a self-test and discovers that its internal memory checksum does not match the expected value. This indicates that the data stored in the module's ROM or EEPROM has become corrupted. The checksum is a verification method used to ensure data integrity. When the stored checksum does not match the calculated checksum, the module knows its programming or calibration data has been compromised. This can lead to erratic engine behavior, poor performance, or a no-start condition.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may exhibit unpredictable behavior including stalling, misfires, or failure to start. Driving with a compromised control module can be unsafe.

causes.cause: Failed or failing PCM/ECM (internal hardware failure)
Corrupted PCM software or failed reprogramming attempt
Low battery voltage or power supply issues to the PCM
Water intrusion or physical damage to the PCM

symptoms: Engine may not start or stalls intermittently

fixSteps.title: Verify the code
fixSteps.instruction: Use an OBD-II scanner to confirm P0601 is present. Clear the code and see if it returns. Check for any Technical Service Bulletins (TSBs) for your specific vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Check PCM power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the PCM power supply and ground circuits. Verify battery voltage is at least 12.4V with engine off. Check for corroded or loose connections at the PCM harness.

fixSteps.title: Inspect PCM for physical damage
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the PCM and inspect for signs of water damage, corrosion, burnt components, or physical damage to the circuit board.

fixSteps.title: Reprogram or replace PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Attempt to reflash the PCM with the latest software calibration using a factory scan tool. If reprogramming fails or the code returns, the PCM must be replaced and programmed to the vehicle.

faq.q: Can I drive with code P0601?
faq.a: It is not recommended. A corrupted PCM can cause unpredictable engine behavior, stalling, or a no-start condition. Have the vehicle diagnosed as soon as possible.

faq.q: Can a low battery cause P0601?
faq.a: Yes, a weak battery or poor electrical connections can cause voltage fluctuations that corrupt the PCM memory, triggering this code.

faq.q: Does P0601 always mean the PCM needs replacement?
faq.a: Not always. Sometimes a reflash or software update can resolve the issue. However, if the internal hardware has failed, replacement is necessary.

title: Control Module Programming Error
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a programming error, meaning the software or calibration data is not correctly loaded or is incompatible.
fullDescription: Code P0602 is triggered when the PCM determines that its internal programming is incorrect, incomplete, or incompatible with the vehicle. This often occurs after a failed reprogramming attempt, when the wrong calibration file is loaded, or when an aftermarket tune causes a conflict. The PCM relies on precise calibration data to manage fuel delivery, ignition timing, and emissions. Without correct programming, the engine cannot operate properly.
safetyImpact: Engine may run poorly or not start at all. Incorrect calibration data can cause dangerous operating conditions including overheating or detonation.

causes.cause: Wrong calibration file loaded during programming

fixSteps.title: Verify and document codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan the vehicle and record all stored DTCs. Research TSBs for your vehicle that may address programming updates.

fixSteps.title: Check for recent service work
fixSteps.instruction: Determine if the PCM was recently reprogrammed or if any aftermarket tuning was performed. Revert to factory calibration if an aftermarket tune was applied.

fixSteps.title: Reprogram the PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Using a factory-level scan tool or J2534 pass-through device, reprogram the PCM with the correct and latest calibration file from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Replace PCM if needed
fixSteps.instruction: If reprogramming fails repeatedly, the PCM may have a hardware fault and must be replaced. The new module must be programmed to the vehicle and the immobilizer must be relearned.

faq.q: What causes a PCM programming error?
faq.a: Most commonly, an interrupted reflash, wrong calibration file, power loss during programming, or an incompatible aftermarket tune.

faq.q: Can I fix P0602 myself?
faq.a: PCM reprogramming typically requires a factory-level scan tool and the correct calibration files. This is generally best left to a dealer or professional technician.

faq.q: Will clearing the code fix P0602?
faq.a: No. Simply clearing the code does not fix the underlying programming issue. The PCM must be properly reprogrammed with the correct software.

title: Internal Control Module KAM Error
shortDescription: The PCM has detected an error in its Keep Alive Memory (KAM), which stores adaptive learning values and long-term fuel trim data.
fullDescription: Code P0603 indicates the Powertrain Control Module has detected a problem with its Keep Alive Memory. KAM is a section of the PCM's memory that retains learned values even when the ignition is off, including long-term fuel trims, idle speed adjustments, and transmission shift adaptation. When this memory is lost or corrupted, the PCM must relearn these values, which can cause temporary driveability issues. Frequent loss of KAM data may indicate a deeper electrical problem.
safetyImpact: Generally safe to drive short-term, but the vehicle may exhibit rough idle, poor shifting, and reduced fuel economy until adaptive values are relearned.

causes.cause: Faulty PCM power supply circuit or blown fuse

fixSteps.title: Check battery and connections
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the battery for proper charge (12.4V+ with engine off, 13.5-14.5V running). Clean battery terminals and ensure tight connections.

fixSteps.title: Inspect PCM fuses and power supply
fixSteps.instruction: Check fuses related to PCM power. Verify constant power supply to the PCM using a wiring diagram for your vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Clear code and drive
fixSteps.instruction: Clear the DTC and drive the vehicle through a normal drive cycle. If the code does not return, the issue was likely a one-time battery event. Allow the PCM to relearn adaptive values.

fixSteps.title: Diagnose PCM if code returns
fixSteps.instruction: If the code keeps returning, test the PCM constant power circuit with a multimeter. If power is stable, the PCM may need replacement.

faq.q: Will P0603 clear on its own?
faq.a: It often does after a battery replacement or reconnection. Clear the code and drive. If it does not return, no further action is needed.

faq.q: Is P0603 serious?
faq.a: By itself, not usually. It often results from routine battery service. However, if it recurs, it may indicate a wiring or PCM issue.

faq.q: Why did I get P0603 after replacing my battery?
faq.a: Disconnecting the battery erases the PCM's Keep Alive Memory. The PCM detects this loss and sets the code. It will relearn values as you drive.

title: Internal Control Module RAM Error
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a Random Access Memory (RAM) error during its internal self-test.
fullDescription: Code P0604 is set when the PCM detects a fault within its internal RAM during a self-diagnostic test. RAM is the working memory the PCM uses in real-time to process sensor inputs, perform calculations, and control engine and transmission outputs. A RAM failure means the PCM cannot reliably process data, which can lead to severe driveability problems or complete engine shutdown. Unlike KAM errors, a RAM fault is typically indicative of a hardware failure within the PCM itself.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may stall, misfire, or fail to start. Unreliable PCM calculations can cause dangerous driving conditions.

causes.cause: Voltage spikes or electrical surges damaging PCM circuits
Water intrusion or corrosion inside the PCM
Extreme heat exposure causing PCM component degradation

symptoms: Loss of communication with PCM during scan

fixSteps.title: Confirm the code and check for TSBs
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for codes and confirm P0604. Check for manufacturer TSBs that may address known PCM issues for your vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Verify PCM power and ground
fixSteps.instruction: Test PCM power and ground circuits to ensure stable voltage. Check for voltage drops in ground circuits. Repair any wiring issues found.

fixSteps.title: Inspect PCM for damage
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and inspect the PCM for signs of water, corrosion, heat damage, or burnt components on the circuit board.

fixSteps.title: Replace the PCM
fixSteps.instruction: P0604 almost always requires PCM replacement. The new PCM must be programmed with the correct calibration and the immobilizer/security system must be relearned.

faq.q: Can P0604 be fixed without replacing the PCM?
faq.a: In rare cases, a reflash may help, but P0604 typically indicates a hardware failure in the PCM's RAM and requires replacement.

faq.q: How much does it cost to replace a PCM?
faq.a: A new or remanufactured PCM typically costs $300-$1000 for the part, plus $100-$500 for programming and installation.

faq.q: Can a bad ground cause P0604?
faq.a: It is unlikely but not impossible. A bad ground can cause voltage instability that may trigger false RAM errors. Always verify power and ground first.

title: Internal Control Module ROM Error
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a Read Only Memory (ROM) error, indicating the permanently stored programming data is corrupted.
fullDescription: Code P0605 is set when the PCM detects an error in its Read Only Memory during self-testing. ROM contains the core firmware and calibration data that the PCM uses to operate. Unlike RAM, ROM data is not supposed to change during normal operation. A ROM error indicates that the foundational programming of the PCM has become corrupted or is no longer readable, which prevents the module from functioning correctly. This is a critical fault that typically requires PCM replacement.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start or may exhibit severe performance issues. The PCM cannot reliably control engine functions with corrupted ROM.

causes.cause: Failed software update or reflash attempt

symptoms: Engine will not start or cranks but fails to run
PCM may not communicate with scan tool

fixSteps.title: Confirm code and check communications
fixSteps.instruction: Attempt to communicate with the PCM using an OBD-II scanner. Record all stored codes. Check for manufacturer TSBs.

fixSteps.title: Verify electrical supply to PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Test the battery and alternator output. Verify PCM power supply and ground circuits for proper voltage and continuity.

fixSteps.title: Attempt PCM reflash
fixSteps.instruction: Try to reprogram the PCM with the latest manufacturer software. If the module will not accept programming, it has failed.

fixSteps.title: Replace the PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new or remanufactured PCM. Program it with the correct calibration for the vehicle. Perform security/immobilizer relearn procedure.

faq.q: What is ROM in the PCM?
faq.a: ROM (Read Only Memory) stores the permanent firmware and calibration data the PCM needs to operate the engine and transmission. It does not normally change during operation.

faq.q: Can I continue driving with P0605?
faq.a: Generally no. A ROM error usually causes severe driveability issues or a no-start condition. The vehicle should be towed to a shop.

faq.q: Is P0605 always a PCM failure?
faq.a: In most cases, yes. While a reflash may occasionally resolve the issue, ROM errors typically indicate irreversible hardware damage.

title: ECM/PCM Processor Fault
shortDescription: The PCM's internal processor has failed a self-test, indicating the main processing unit is not functioning correctly.
fullDescription: Code P0606 is set when the ECM or PCM internal processor fails its self-diagnostic test. The processor is the brain of the control module, responsible for executing all calculations related to fuel injection, ignition timing, emissions control, and transmission operation. When the processor itself is faulty, the entire control module is compromised. This code may be intermittent if the processor is degrading or may be persistent if it has fully failed.
safetyImpact: Critical safety concern. A faulty processor can cause unpredictable engine behavior including stalling at highway speeds, loss of power steering assist, and failure to start.

causes.cause: Overheating of the PCM due to location or cooling issues

fixSteps.title: Scan and document all codes
fixSteps.instruction: Record all DTCs and freeze frame data. Note if the code is intermittent or persistent. Check for TSBs and recalls.

fixSteps.title: Inspect PCM wiring and connectors
fixSteps.instruction: Check all PCM connectors for bent pins, corrosion, or loose terminals. Inspect wiring harness for chafing or damage.

fixSteps.title: Test PCM power and grounds
fixSteps.instruction: Verify stable voltage at all PCM power pins. Check all ground circuits for excessive resistance using a voltage drop test.

fixSteps.title: Replace the PCM
fixSteps.instruction: If wiring checks out, replace the PCM. Ensure the replacement is correctly programmed with the latest calibration. Perform all required relearn procedures.

faq.q: Is P0606 a serious code?
faq.a: Yes. It indicates the PCM's main processor is failing, which affects all engine and transmission control functions.

faq.q: Can a loose connection cause P0606?
faq.a: Rarely, but poor connections to the PCM can cause voltage instability that may trigger processor faults. Always check wiring before condemning the PCM.

faq.q: How common is P0606?
faq.a: It is relatively uncommon but can occur in vehicles with high mileage or those exposed to harsh environmental conditions.

title: Control Module Performance
shortDescription: The PCM has detected that its overall performance does not meet expected parameters during internal diagnostics.
fullDescription: Code P0607 is a general code indicating the PCM has failed an internal performance test. This differs from specific memory or processor fault codes in that it represents an overall performance degradation rather than a specific component failure. The PCM continuously monitors its own performance and sets this code when it determines it cannot reliably perform its required functions. This may be caused by internal component degradation, power supply issues, or environmental factors affecting the module.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may operate unreliably. Engine stalling, misfires, and transmission issues are possible. Should be diagnosed promptly.

causes.cause: Unstable power supply to the PCM
PCM overheating due to environmental conditions

fixSteps.title: Scan and check for TSBs
fixSteps.instruction: Read all DTCs and freeze frame data. Check manufacturer TSBs as some vehicles have known software updates that address P0607.

fixSteps.title: Test PCM power circuits
fixSteps.instruction: Verify battery condition and charging system. Test all PCM power and ground circuits for proper voltage and minimal resistance.

fixSteps.title: Attempt software update
fixSteps.instruction: Reprogram the PCM with the latest available software from the manufacturer. Many P0607 cases are resolved by software updates.

fixSteps.title: Replace PCM if necessary
fixSteps.instruction: If a software update does not resolve the issue, replace the PCM with a new or remanufactured unit and program it accordingly.

faq.q: Can a software update fix P0607?
faq.a: Yes, in many cases. Manufacturers frequently release updated calibrations that resolve PCM performance monitoring issues.

faq.q: Is P0607 the same as a bad PCM?
faq.a: Not necessarily. While it can indicate PCM hardware failure, it may also be caused by power supply issues or outdated software.

faq.q: Should I drive with P0607?
faq.a: Limit driving. The vehicle may stall or behave unpredictably. Have it diagnosed soon.

title: Starter Relay Circuit
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the starter relay control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0615 is set when the PCM detects an abnormal condition in the starter motor relay circuit. In modern vehicles, the PCM controls or monitors the starter relay to manage engine cranking. The PCM expects specific voltage levels in this circuit during cranking and non-cranking conditions. If the voltage reading does not match the expected value, this code is stored. The fault could be in the relay itself, the wiring between the PCM and the relay, or the PCM's driver circuit.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may fail to start or may have difficulty starting. In rare cases, the starter may engage unexpectedly.

causes.cause: Open or shorted wiring in the starter relay circuit
Corroded or loose connections at the relay or PCM

symptoms: Engine cranks slowly or does not crank
Clicking noise when turning the key

fixSteps.title: Check the starter relay
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the starter relay in the fuse/relay box. Swap it with an identical relay to test. Listen for clicking when turning the key.

fixSteps.title: Test the relay circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Using a multimeter, verify power supply to the relay coil and control signal from the PCM. Check for proper ground at the relay.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the wiring between the PCM and the starter relay for damage, chafing, corrosion, or shorts. Repair as needed.

fixSteps.title: Replace relay or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty starter relay or repair damaged wiring. Clear the code and test for proper starting.

faq.q: Can a bad starter cause P0615?
faq.a: Not directly. P0615 refers to the relay circuit, not the starter motor itself. However, a shorted starter could damage the relay circuit.

faq.q: Where is the starter relay located?
faq.a: Typically in the underhood fuse/relay box. Consult your owner's manual or a service manual for the exact location.

faq.q: Is P0615 an emergency?
faq.a: Not usually an emergency, but it can cause no-start conditions. Address it promptly to avoid being stranded.

title: Starter Relay Circuit Low
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a low voltage condition in the starter relay control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0616 indicates the PCM has measured lower than expected voltage in the starter relay circuit. This typically means there is excessive resistance in the circuit, a poor ground connection, or the relay control wire is shorted to ground. The low voltage prevents the relay from properly energizing, which can lead to starting difficulties or failure.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may not start reliably. Typically does not present an immediate driving hazard if the engine is already running.

causes.cause: Shorted wiring in the starter relay circuit (short to ground)
Faulty starter relay with internal short

fixSteps.title: Test the starter relay
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the starter relay and test it with a multimeter for coil resistance and contact continuity. Replace if out of specification.

fixSteps.title: Check for short to ground
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the relay and PCM connector. Test the control wire for continuity to ground. It should be open (no continuity) with both ends disconnected.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Trace the wiring harness between the PCM and relay. Look for damaged insulation, pinched wires, or areas where the wire may be contacting metal. Repair as needed.

fixSteps.title: Test PCM output
fixSteps.instruction: If wiring is good and relay is new, test the PCM control output with a test light or oscilloscope to verify proper signal.

faq.q: What does 'circuit low' mean for P0616?
faq.a: It means the PCM is reading lower voltage than expected in the starter relay control circuit, often indicating a short to ground or excessive resistance.

faq.q: Can I just replace the relay?
faq.a: Start with the relay as it is the most common and cheapest fix. If the code returns, further circuit diagnosis is needed.

faq.q: Will P0616 leave me stranded?
faq.a: Possibly. If the starter relay cannot energize, the engine will not crank. Have the issue repaired promptly.

title: Starter Relay Circuit High
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a high voltage condition in the starter relay control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0617 is set when the PCM detects higher than expected voltage in the starter relay control circuit. This could mean the control wire is shorted to battery voltage, the relay is stuck engaged, or there is a wiring issue causing unwanted voltage on the circuit. A high voltage condition can cause the starter to engage unexpectedly or remain engaged, which can damage the starter motor and flywheel.
safetyImpact: The starter may engage unexpectedly or remain engaged after the engine starts, which can damage the starter, flywheel, and be a safety hazard.

causes.cause: Wiring shorted to battery voltage in the relay circuit
Starter relay stuck in the engaged position
Faulty ignition switch holding the start signal

symptoms: Starter remains engaged after engine starts (grinding noise)

fixSteps.title: Disconnect the starter relay immediately
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the starter relay from the fuse box to prevent starter damage. Test the relay for stuck contacts.

fixSteps.title: Test for short to power
fixSteps.instruction: With the relay removed, test the control wire at the relay socket for unexpected voltage. There should be no voltage with the key in the off position.

fixSteps.title: Inspect ignition switch and wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the ignition switch for proper operation. Inspect all wiring in the starter relay circuit for damage or shorts to power sources.

fixSteps.title: Repair and verify
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty relay, repair any shorted wiring, or replace the ignition switch as needed. Install the relay and verify proper starter operation.

faq.q: Can a stuck starter relay damage my engine?
faq.a: Yes. A relay stuck in the on position will keep the starter engaged, which can damage the starter motor, flywheel ring gear, and associated wiring.

faq.q: Why is my starter grinding after the engine starts?
faq.a: This likely means the starter relay is stuck or the control circuit is holding the starter engaged. Remove the relay immediately and diagnose.

faq.q: Is P0617 urgent?
faq.a: Yes. An engaged starter on a running engine can cause significant damage. Address this issue immediately.

title: Generator Control Circuit
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the generator (alternator) control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0620 is set when the PCM detects an issue with the circuit that controls the generator (alternator) output. Modern vehicles use the PCM to regulate alternator charging output based on electrical load, battery state of charge, and operating conditions. The PCM communicates with the alternator's voltage regulator through a control circuit. When this circuit malfunctions, the alternator may overcharge, undercharge, or not charge at all.
safetyImpact: The battery may discharge while driving, leading to eventual stalling. Overcharging can damage electrical components and the battery.

causes.cause: Faulty alternator or internal voltage regulator
Open or shorted wiring in the generator control circuit
Poor connection at the alternator or PCM connector

symptoms: Check Engine Light and/or battery warning light illuminated
Vehicle stalls due to dead battery

fixSteps.title: Test charging system
fixSteps.instruction: Check battery voltage with engine off (should be 12.4V+) and running (should be 13.5-14.5V). If charging voltage is out of range, proceed with diagnosis.

fixSteps.title: Inspect alternator connections
fixSteps.instruction: Check all wiring connections at the alternator for corrosion, looseness, or damage. Inspect the control signal wire from the PCM.

fixSteps.title: Test the control circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Using a wiring diagram, test the control signal wire between the PCM and alternator for continuity and proper resistance. Check for shorts.

fixSteps.title: Replace alternator if needed
fixSteps.instruction: If the control circuit wiring is good, the alternator's internal voltage regulator has likely failed. Replace the alternator, clear codes, and verify proper charging.

faq.q: Can I drive with P0620?
faq.a: For short distances, but the battery may drain. If the battery light is on, limit driving to essential trips and have the system diagnosed promptly.

faq.q: What controls the alternator output?
faq.a: In modern vehicles, the PCM sends a duty cycle signal to the alternator's voltage regulator to control charging output based on demand.

faq.q: Is P0620 always the alternator?
faq.a: Not always. The wiring between the PCM and alternator can also cause this code. Test the circuit before replacing parts.

title: Generator Field Terminal Circuit Low
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a low voltage condition on the generator (alternator) field terminal circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0625 is set when the PCM detects lower than expected voltage on the alternator field terminal circuit. The field terminal is used by the PCM to control the strength of the alternator's magnetic field, which directly controls charging output. A low voltage reading suggests a short to ground, excessive resistance, or a failed alternator field winding.
safetyImpact: The alternator may not charge properly, leading to battery drain and potential stalling. Electrical systems may operate intermittently.

causes.cause: Faulty alternator field winding or regulator
Short to ground in the field terminal wiring
Corroded or damaged connector at the alternator

symptoms: Check Engine Light and battery light illuminated
Dim headlights and weak electrical accessories
Battery going dead overnight or during short trips

fixSteps.title: Check charging voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Measure voltage at the battery with the engine running. Normal is 13.5-14.5V. Low voltage confirms a charging issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect alternator connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check the alternator connector for corrosion, bent pins, or damage. Clean and repair as needed.

fixSteps.title: Test field terminal circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the alternator and test the field terminal wire for shorts to ground. Check resistance of the alternator field winding.

fixSteps.title: Replace alternator or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the alternator if the field winding is faulty. Repair any shorted wiring found in the circuit.

faq.q: What is the generator field terminal?
faq.a: It is the circuit through which the PCM controls the alternator's electromagnetic field strength, regulating how much the alternator charges.

faq.q: Can a bad battery cause P0625?
faq.a: A bad battery will not directly cause P0625, but it can mask the symptoms. Always test the battery separately.

faq.q: How do I test the alternator field winding?
faq.a: With the alternator removed, measure resistance across the field winding terminals with a multimeter. Compare to the manufacturer's specification.

title: Fuel Pump 'A' Control Circuit Open
shortDescription: The PCM has detected an open circuit in the fuel pump relay or control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0627 is set when the PCM detects an open circuit in the fuel pump control circuit. The PCM energizes the fuel pump relay to power the fuel pump when the engine is running or during cranking. An open circuit means the relay is not receiving the control signal, which will prevent the fuel pump from operating. Without fuel pump operation, the engine cannot run.
safetyImpact: Vehicle will likely not start or will stall while driving due to loss of fuel delivery. Being stranded in traffic is a safety concern.

causes.cause: Open wiring between PCM and fuel pump relay
PCM fuel pump driver circuit failure

symptoms: Engine cranks but does not start
Engine stalls and will not restart
No fuel pump prime sound when key is turned to ON

fixSteps.title: Check fuel pump fuse and relay
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the fuel pump fuse and relay in the fuse box. Check the fuse for continuity. Swap the relay with a known good identical relay to test.

fixSteps.title: Test for relay control signal
fixSteps.instruction: Using a test light or multimeter, check for a control signal at the relay socket when the key is turned to ON. No signal indicates an open circuit upstream.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring for open circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Trace the control wire from the relay socket to the PCM. Check for breaks, corroded connectors, or disconnected terminals. Test continuity end-to-end.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring or replace relay
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any broken wiring found. Replace the fuel pump relay if faulty. Clear codes and verify the fuel pump operates when the key is turned on.

faq.q: Can I hear if my fuel pump is working?
faq.a: Yes. Turn the key to ON (not start) and listen near the fuel tank. You should hear a brief humming sound as the pump primes. No sound suggests the pump is not receiving power.

faq.q: Is the fuel pump relay the same as the fuel pump?
faq.a: No. The relay is an electrical switch that supplies power to the fuel pump. The pump is in or near the fuel tank.

faq.q: What does 'open circuit' mean?
faq.a: An open circuit means the electrical path is broken somewhere, like a cut wire or bad connection, preventing current from flowing.

title: Fuel Pump 'A' Control Circuit Low
shortDescription: The PCM has detected low voltage in the fuel pump control circuit, indicating a short to ground or excessive resistance.
fullDescription: Code P0628 is set when the PCM detects lower than expected voltage in the fuel pump relay control circuit. This typically indicates the control wire is shorted to ground or there is excessive resistance in the circuit. The fuel pump may still operate intermittently or at reduced capacity, but reliable fuel delivery is compromised.
safetyImpact: Fuel pump may operate intermittently causing stalling. Loss of fuel delivery while driving is a safety concern.

causes.cause: Wiring shorted to ground in the fuel pump relay circuit
Faulty fuel pump relay with internal short
Corroded wiring or connectors causing high resistance

fixSteps.title: Test fuel pump relay
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the relay and test coil resistance and contact operation with a multimeter. Replace if out of specification.

fixSteps.title: Check for short to ground
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the relay and PCM connector. Test the control wire for continuity to ground. There should be none with both ends disconnected.

fixSteps.title: Repair shorted wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Locate and repair any areas where the wiring insulation is damaged and the wire is contacting ground. Use heat shrink tubing for repairs.

fixSteps.title: Verify repair
fixSteps.instruction: Reconnect all components, clear codes, and verify proper fuel pump operation. Monitor the fuel pump circuit voltage with a scan tool.

faq.q: What does circuit low mean for P0628?
faq.a: It means the PCM is measuring lower voltage than expected on the fuel pump relay control wire, usually due to a short to ground.

faq.q: Can a bad fuel pump cause P0628?
faq.a: Not directly. P0628 refers to the relay control circuit, not the fuel pump motor circuit. However, a shorted fuel pump can blow fuses.

faq.q: Is P0628 dangerous?
faq.a: It can be if it causes the fuel pump to stop while driving. Have it repaired promptly.

title: Fuel Pump 'A' Control Circuit High
shortDescription: The PCM has detected high voltage in the fuel pump control circuit, indicating a short to power.
fullDescription: Code P0629 is set when the PCM detects higher than expected voltage on the fuel pump relay control circuit. This typically means the control wire is shorted to a power source. This can cause the fuel pump to run continuously even when the engine is off, which is a fire hazard and will drain the battery. The condition must be addressed immediately.
safetyImpact: Fuel pump may run continuously, creating a potential fire hazard. The fuel system may be overpressurized, and the battery will drain.

causes.cause: Wiring shorted to battery voltage in the relay control circuit
Fuel pump relay stuck in the closed position
Aftermarket wiring modifications causing power feedback

symptoms: Fuel pump runs continuously even with key off

fixSteps.title: Remove fuel pump relay
fixSteps.instruction: Immediately remove the fuel pump relay to stop continuous pump operation. This prevents fire risk and battery drain.

fixSteps.title: Test for power on control wire
fixSteps.instruction: With the relay removed and key off, test the relay control socket for voltage. There should be no voltage on the control pin with the key off.

fixSteps.title: Trace short to power
fixSteps.instruction: If voltage is present on the control wire with the key off, trace the wire for areas where it may be shorted to a power source. Check for aftermarket wiring.

fixSteps.title: Repair and verify
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the shorted wiring or replace the stuck relay. Reinstall the relay and verify the fuel pump only runs with the key on and stops with the key off.

faq.q: Is a continuously running fuel pump dangerous?
faq.a: Yes. It can overpressurize the fuel system, create fire risk, and will drain the battery. Remove the fuel pump relay as a temporary fix.

faq.q: Can a stuck relay be the problem?
faq.a: Absolutely. A relay with welded contacts will keep the fuel pump powered regardless of the PCM command. Replace it immediately.

faq.q: Why is there voltage on the fuel pump circuit with the key off?
faq.a: A wire shorted to a constant power source, a stuck relay, or aftermarket wiring can supply unwanted power to the fuel pump circuit.

title: VIN Not Programmed or Incompatible - ECM/PCM
shortDescription: The PCM has detected that no Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) is programmed or the stored VIN does not match the vehicle.
fullDescription: Code P0630 is set when the PCM detects that the VIN stored in its memory is missing or does not match the vehicle it is installed in. The VIN is programmed into the PCM during manufacturing or during module replacement. If the VIN is absent or mismatched, the PCM may restrict certain functions including emissions readiness monitors and may prevent the vehicle from passing emissions testing.
safetyImpact: Vehicle will typically run normally but may not pass emissions testing. Some functions may be restricted.

causes.cause: PCM replaced and not properly programmed with the vehicle VIN
Used PCM installed from another vehicle
VIN data corrupted in PCM memory

fixSteps.title: Verify the code
fixSteps.instruction: Confirm P0630 with an OBD-II scanner. Read the VIN stored in the PCM and compare it to the VIN on the vehicle's doorjamb sticker or dashboard plate.

fixSteps.title: Check PCM history
fixSteps.instruction: Determine if the PCM was recently replaced or reprogrammed. If a used PCM was installed, it likely has the wrong VIN.

fixSteps.title: Program VIN into PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Using a factory scan tool or equivalent, program the correct VIN into the PCM. This typically requires a subscription to the manufacturer's reprogramming service.

fixSteps.title: Clear codes and verify
fixSteps.instruction: After programming the VIN, clear all codes and verify the code does not return. Run a drive cycle to complete emissions monitors.

faq.q: Can I drive with P0630?
faq.a: Yes, the vehicle will usually drive normally. However, you may not pass emissions testing until the VIN is programmed correctly.

faq.q: Why does the PCM need the VIN?
faq.a: The VIN is used by the PCM for emissions compliance reporting, security features, and ensuring the correct calibration is being used.

faq.q: Can I program the VIN myself?
faq.a: On some vehicles, a J2534 device and manufacturer subscription can be used. On others, it requires a dealer-level tool.

title: A/C Clutch Relay Control Circuit
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the air conditioning compressor clutch relay control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0645 is set when the PCM detects an abnormal condition in the A/C compressor clutch relay control circuit. The PCM controls the A/C compressor engagement through a relay to manage compressor cycling, prevent engagement during high engine load, and protect against low refrigerant conditions. A fault in this circuit means the A/C system may not engage or may engage at inappropriate times.
safetyImpact: Air conditioning will not function, which can be uncomfortable but is not a safety hazard in most conditions. Defrost mode may also be affected.

causes.cause: Open or shorted wiring in the relay control circuit
Low refrigerant causing the PCM to disable A/C
PCM A/C relay driver circuit failure

symptoms: Defrost function reduced (uses A/C for dehumidification)
A/C compressor clutch does not click on

fixSteps.title: Test A/C clutch relay
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the A/C relay in the fuse box. Swap with an identical relay to test. Listen for the compressor clutch engaging when A/C is turned on.

fixSteps.title: Check refrigerant level
fixSteps.instruction: Use an A/C gauge set to check system pressure. Low refrigerant will prevent the PCM from engaging the compressor. Verify pressures are within specification.

fixSteps.title: Test relay control signal
fixSteps.instruction: With the A/C on, check for a control signal at the relay socket from the PCM. No signal indicates a wiring or PCM issue.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring and repair
fixSteps.instruction: Trace and test the wiring between the PCM and the A/C relay. Repair any damaged wiring. Replace the relay or compressor clutch coil as needed.

faq.q: Is P0645 just about the A/C?
faq.a: Yes, it relates to the A/C compressor clutch relay circuit. It does not affect engine operation but will disable the air conditioning.

faq.q: Can low Freon cause P0645?
faq.a: Indirectly. Low refrigerant may prevent compressor engagement, but P0645 specifically indicates a circuit fault, not a pressure issue.

faq.q: Will my car overheat without A/C?
faq.a: No. The engine cooling system is separate from the A/C system. However, the A/C condenser fan may also be controlled by this relay on some vehicles.

title: Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) Control Circuit
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (Check Engine Light) control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0650 is set when the PCM detects a problem with the circuit that controls the Check Engine Light (MIL) on the instrument cluster. The PCM controls this light by grounding its circuit. If the PCM detects that the circuit is open, shorted, or not responding as expected, it stores P0650. Ironically, this code means the Check Engine Light itself may not illuminate when other fault codes are present, potentially hiding other issues from the driver.
safetyImpact: The Check Engine Light may not illuminate when other problems exist, leaving the driver unaware of engine or emissions issues.

causes.cause: Burnt out MIL bulb or failed LED in the instrument cluster
Open wiring between PCM and instrument cluster

symptoms: Check Engine Light does not illuminate during key-on bulb check
Check Engine Light stays on constantly regardless of codes
Other warning lights may also malfunction
Vehicle may fail emissions inspection (MIL function check)

fixSteps.title: Check bulb during key-on
fixSteps.instruction: Turn the key to ON without starting. All warning lights should briefly illuminate. If the Check Engine Light does not come on, the bulb or circuit is faulty.

fixSteps.title: Inspect the instrument cluster
fixSteps.instruction: Check if the MIL bulb is burnt out (older vehicles). On newer vehicles with LED clusters, the cluster itself may need diagnosis.

fixSteps.title: Test the MIL circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Using a wiring diagram, test the circuit between the PCM and instrument cluster for continuity and proper ground signal from the PCM.

fixSteps.title: Repair or replace components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the burnt bulb, repair wiring, or replace the instrument cluster as needed. Clear codes and verify the MIL illuminates during key-on check.

faq.q: Is P0650 serious?
faq.a: It is important because a non-functional Check Engine Light can hide other problems. You may not know about engine or emissions faults.

faq.q: Will P0650 cause my car to fail emissions?
faq.a: Yes. Most emissions tests verify the MIL illuminates during the key-on bulb check. A non-functional MIL will fail this test.

faq.q: How would I know about P0650 if my Check Engine Light does not work?
faq.a: You may discover it during a routine scan, emissions test, or when diagnosing other symptoms.

title: Engine RPM Output Circuit
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the engine RPM output signal circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0654 is set when the PCM detects a fault in the circuit that outputs the engine RPM signal to other modules or the instrument cluster tachometer. The PCM generates an RPM output signal based on crankshaft position sensor data. If this output circuit has an open, short, or other fault, the tachometer may not work and other modules that rely on the RPM signal may malfunction.
safetyImpact: The tachometer may not function, and other modules may not receive engine speed data. Driving is generally safe but monitoring RPM becomes difficult.

causes.cause: Open or shorted wiring in the RPM output circuit
Corroded or loose connector at the PCM or cluster

symptoms: Tachometer not working or reading erratically
Other modules may report RPM-related faults

fixSteps.title: Verify tachometer operation
fixSteps.instruction: Start the engine and observe the tachometer. If it does not move or reads erratically, the RPM output circuit is likely at fault.

fixSteps.title: Check RPM output circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Using a wiring diagram, locate the RPM output wire from the PCM. Test for a signal at the PCM connector with the engine running.

fixSteps.title: Test wiring continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Check the RPM output wire for continuity from the PCM to the instrument cluster or destination module. Check for shorts to ground or power.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring or components
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any damaged wiring. If the signal is present at the PCM but not at the cluster, the wiring is faulty. If no signal at the PCM, the PCM output driver may have failed.

faq.q: Can I drive without a tachometer?
faq.a: Yes, but you will not be able to monitor engine RPM visually. Automatic transmission vehicles are less affected than manual transmission vehicles.

faq.q: Does P0654 affect engine performance?
faq.a: Not directly. The engine operates based on internal sensor data, not the tachometer output. However, other modules that need RPM data may be affected.

faq.q: Could this be a cluster problem instead?
faq.a: Yes. A faulty instrument cluster can also cause a non-working tachometer. Test the circuit before assuming a PCM fault.

title: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit - Bank 1
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the intake manifold tuning valve (IMTV) control circuit for bank 1.
fullDescription: Code P0660 is set when the PCM detects an electrical fault in the intake manifold tuning valve control circuit. The IMTV (also called a runner control valve or intake manifold runner control) changes the effective length or volume of the intake manifold runners to optimize airflow at different engine speeds. By varying intake runner length, the engine can produce better torque at low speeds and better power at high speeds. A fault in this circuit prevents proper valve operation.
safetyImpact: Engine performance may be reduced, particularly at certain RPM ranges. The vehicle is generally safe to drive.

causes.cause: Faulty intake manifold tuning valve actuator/solenoid
Open or shorted wiring in the IMTV circuit
Stuck intake manifold tuning valve (carbon buildup)

symptoms: Reduced engine performance in certain RPM ranges
Possible rough idle or stumble during acceleration
Rattling noise from intake manifold area

fixSteps.title: Locate and inspect IMTV actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Find the intake manifold tuning valve actuator on the intake manifold. Inspect the connector for corrosion or damage.

fixSteps.title: Test actuator electrically
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the actuator and measure coil resistance with a multimeter. Compare to manufacturer specifications. Also check for power and ground at the connector.

fixSteps.title: Check valve operation
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the valve for free movement. Carbon buildup can cause it to stick. Clean if stuck. Apply 12V directly to the actuator to test operation.

fixSteps.title: Replace actuator or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty actuator or repair damaged wiring. Clear codes and verify proper operation.

faq.q: What does the intake manifold tuning valve do?
faq.a: It varies the effective length of the intake runners to optimize airflow. Short runners help at high RPM, while long runners help at low RPM.

faq.q: Can I drive with P0660?
faq.a: Yes, but you may notice reduced performance. The engine will not be damaged but will not perform optimally.

faq.q: Why does carbon buildup affect this valve?
faq.a: Carbon from the PCV system and intake gases can accumulate on the valve, causing it to stick open or closed.

title: ECM/PCM Power Relay Control Circuit Open
shortDescription: The PCM has detected an open circuit in its own power relay control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0685 is set when the PCM detects an open circuit in the relay that provides its main power supply. The PCM power relay (sometimes called the main relay or ECM relay) supplies power to the PCM and often to other engine management components. When the PCM cannot control this relay properly, it may shut down unexpectedly or fail to power up. This is a critical circuit since the PCM needs stable power to operate.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may stall unexpectedly or fail to start. Loss of PCM power means complete loss of engine management.

causes.cause: Open wiring in the relay control circuit
Corroded or loose relay socket connections
Blown fuse in the relay power supply

symptoms: Check Engine Light illuminated (when PCM has power)
Vehicle stalls and will not restart

fixSteps.title: Locate and test the PCM power relay
fixSteps.instruction: Find the PCM main relay in the fuse/relay box. Test the relay by swapping with an identical relay. Check related fuses.

fixSteps.title: Test relay control circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Check for control signal at the relay socket when the key is turned on. Verify power supply to the relay coil.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring for open circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Test the control wire from the relay socket to the PCM for continuity. Check connectors for loose or corroded terminals.

fixSteps.title: Replace relay or repair wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty relay or repair the open wiring. Verify the PCM powers up correctly and stays powered during engine operation.

faq.q: What is the PCM power relay?
faq.a: It is a relay that supplies the main power to the PCM. Without it, the PCM cannot operate and the engine will not run.

faq.q: Can a bad relay cause intermittent stalling?
faq.a: Yes. A relay with worn contacts can lose connection intermittently, causing the PCM to lose power and the engine to stall.

faq.q: How do I find the PCM relay?
faq.a: Check the fuse/relay box diagram on the cover or in the owner's manual. It may be labeled ECM relay, main relay, or PCM relay.

title: ECM/PCM Power Relay Sense Circuit
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a malfunction in the power relay feedback or sense circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0690 is set when the PCM does not receive the expected feedback signal from its power relay. Modern PCMs monitor the relay output through a sense circuit to confirm the relay is functioning correctly. If the sense circuit indicates the relay is not providing the expected output, the PCM stores this code. This is a monitoring circuit issue and may indicate the relay is not closing properly or the sense wire is faulty.
safetyImpact: The vehicle may experience intermittent power loss to the PCM. If the relay actually fails, the engine will stall.

causes.cause: Faulty PCM power relay not closing fully
Open or shorted sense wire between relay and PCM

fixSteps.title: Test the PCM power relay
fixSteps.instruction: Test the relay by swapping with a known good unit. Check relay contacts for resistance and proper switching.

fixSteps.title: Verify relay output
fixSteps.instruction: With the relay installed and key on, verify voltage on the relay output terminal matches battery voltage. Low voltage indicates relay or supply issues.

fixSteps.title: Test the sense circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the sense wire from the relay to the PCM. Test for continuity and verify voltage at the PCM sense pin matches relay output.

fixSteps.title: Repair as needed
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the relay if faulty. Repair or replace the sense wire if damaged. Clean corroded relay socket pins.

faq.q: What is a sense circuit?
faq.a: A sense circuit is a feedback wire that allows the PCM to confirm a relay or component is actually operating as commanded.

faq.q: Can I just ignore P0690?
faq.a: Not recommended. If the relay is failing, it could cause a complete loss of PCM power and sudden engine shutdown.

faq.q: Is P0690 related to the main relay?
faq.a: Yes, it specifically relates to the PCM power relay feedback circuit.

title: Cooling Fan 1 Control Circuit Low
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a low voltage condition in the engine cooling fan 1 control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0691 is set when the PCM detects lower than expected voltage on the cooling fan 1 relay control circuit. The PCM controls the engine cooling fan(s) to maintain proper engine temperature. A low voltage condition suggests the control circuit is shorted to ground, preventing proper fan relay operation. This can lead to engine overheating if the cooling fan cannot be activated when needed.
safetyImpact: Engine may overheat due to cooling fan failure. Overheating can cause severe engine damage including warped heads and blown head gaskets.

causes.cause: Shorted wiring in the cooling fan relay control circuit
Corroded connector at the relay or PCM
Failed cooling fan motor causing excessive current draw

symptoms: Engine overheating, especially at idle or low speed
Cooling fan does not operate or runs constantly

fixSteps.title: Check cooling fan operation
fixSteps.instruction: Start the engine and let it warm up. The cooling fan should engage before the temperature gauge reaches the red zone. Check the fan relay and fuse.

fixSteps.title: Test relay and circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the fan relay and test it. Check the control wire at the relay socket for a short to ground with the relay removed.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring harness
fixSteps.instruction: Trace the control wire from the relay to the PCM. Look for chafed or damaged insulation where the wire may be contacting metal body or engine parts.

fixSteps.title: Repair and verify
fixSteps.instruction: Repair shorted wiring, replace faulty relay, or repair connector. Verify the cooling fan engages at the correct temperature and turns off normally.

faq.q: Can a non-working cooling fan damage my engine?
faq.a: Yes. Without the cooling fan, the engine can overheat quickly, especially at idle or in slow traffic, leading to serious engine damage.

faq.q: Should I stop driving if the engine is overheating?
faq.a: Yes. Pull over safely and turn off the engine. Driving an overheating engine can cause head gasket failure, warped cylinder heads, or complete engine failure.

faq.q: Can I temporarily bypass the relay?
faq.a: As an emergency measure only. A direct-wired fan will run constantly. This is not a permanent fix and can drain the battery.

title: Cooling Fan 1 Control Circuit High
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a high voltage condition in the engine cooling fan 1 control circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0692 indicates the PCM has detected higher than expected voltage in the cooling fan 1 relay control circuit. This may cause the fan to run continuously or at the wrong speed. A high voltage condition typically means the control wire is shorted to a power source or the relay is stuck closed. While a constantly running fan is less dangerous than one that does not run, it wastes energy and can indicate underlying issues.
safetyImpact: The cooling fan may run constantly, draining the battery. If the fan is stuck on high speed, it may cause excessive noise and faster wear.

causes.cause: Wiring shorted to battery voltage in the fan relay circuit
Faulty coolant temperature sensor providing false reading

symptoms: Cooling fan runs constantly, even when engine is cold
Fan continues running after ignition is off
Battery drain from continuous fan operation

fixSteps.title: Verify fan behavior
fixSteps.instruction: Start a cold engine and observe the fan. If it runs immediately on a cold engine, the circuit is likely stuck high.

fixSteps.title: Test the cooling fan relay
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the relay and check if the fan stops. Test the relay for stuck contacts with a multimeter.

fixSteps.title: Check for short to power
fixSteps.instruction: With the relay removed, test the control pin at the relay socket for unexpected voltage with the key off. Trace and repair any short.

fixSteps.title: Replace relay and verify
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new relay. Verify the fan only runs when engine temperature warrants it. Monitor with a scan tool.

faq.q: Is a constantly running fan bad?
faq.a: It causes unnecessary battery drain and accelerated fan motor wear. It also indicates an electrical fault that should be repaired.

faq.q: Can a stuck fan relay drain my battery?
faq.a: Yes. If the fan runs with the key off, it will drain the battery, potentially leaving you stranded.

faq.q: Why does my fan run when the engine is cold?
faq.a: This is abnormal and indicates either a stuck relay, short to power in the control circuit, or a faulty temperature sensor.

title: Transmission Control System (MIL Request)
shortDescription: The Transmission Control Module (TCM) has detected a fault and is requesting the PCM to illuminate the Check Engine Light.
fullDescription: Code P0700 is an informational code that indicates the Transmission Control Module has detected a malfunction and is requesting the MIL be illuminated. P0700 itself does not specify what the transmission fault is. It is accompanied by one or more specific transmission fault codes that identify the actual problem. You must read the specific transmission codes (often stored in the TCM) to determine the root cause of the issue.
safetyImpact: Depends on the accompanying transmission code. The transmission may enter limp mode with limited gear selection. Driving may be impaired.

causes.cause: Specific transmission fault stored in the TCM (see accompanying codes)
Wiring issue between TCM and transmission components

symptoms: Transmission may enter limp or fail-safe mode

fixSteps.title: Read all codes including TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool capable of reading transmission codes. P0700 is just a flag; the actual fault code(s) will be stored in the TCM.

fixSteps.title: Check transmission fluid
fixSteps.instruction: Check transmission fluid level and condition. Low, burnt, or contaminated fluid can cause many transmission codes.
tools: Dipstick or scan tool for sealed transmissions

fixSteps.title: Diagnose the specific transmission code
fixSteps.instruction: Based on the specific codes found, perform targeted diagnosis. Common causes include faulty solenoids, speed sensors, or wiring issues.

fixSteps.title: Repair based on findings
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the specific fault identified by the accompanying codes. This may range from a fluid change to solenoid replacement to transmission overhaul.

faq.q: Does P0700 mean I need a new transmission?
faq.a: Not necessarily. P0700 is just a flag indicating a transmission code exists. The actual problem could be minor (sensor) or major (internal failure).

faq.q: Why does P0700 appear with other codes?
faq.a: P0700 is the TCM's way of asking the PCM to turn on the Check Engine Light. It always accompanies a more specific transmission fault code.

faq.q: Can I drive in limp mode?
faq.a: For short distances to a repair shop, yes. Limp mode limits the transmission to one gear to prevent further damage. Do not drive at highway speeds.

title: Transmission Range Sensor Circuit (PRNDL Input)
shortDescription: The TCM/PCM has detected a malfunction in the transmission range sensor (TRS) circuit, which reports the gear selector position.
fullDescription: Code P0705 is set when the TCM or PCM receives an invalid, irrational, or missing signal from the transmission range sensor (also known as the neutral safety switch or PRNDL sensor). This sensor tells the control module which gear position the driver has selected (Park, Reverse, Neutral, Drive, Low). If the signal is unreliable, the transmission may not shift correctly, the starter may not engage, or the backup lights may not work.
safetyImpact: The vehicle may start in gear (bypassing the neutral safety function) or may not start at all. Incorrect gear indication is a safety concern.

causes.cause: Corroded or damaged wiring at the sensor connector
Low transmission fluid affecting sensor operation

symptoms: Vehicle may not start in Park or Neutral
Transmission shifts erratically or enters limp mode

fixSteps.title: Check shift linkage adjustment
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the shift linkage is properly aligned. The indicated gear position should match the actual transmission position. Adjust if needed.

fixSteps.title: Test transmission range sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Using a multimeter, test the TRS resistance or voltage at each gear position. Values should change smoothly and match specifications.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring and connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check the sensor connector and wiring for corrosion, water intrusion, and damage. The sensor is often exposed to road debris.

fixSteps.title: Replace transmission range sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is faulty, replace it. Ensure proper alignment during installation. Clear codes and test in all gear positions.

faq.q: What is the transmission range sensor?
faq.a: It is a sensor on the transmission that tells the PCM/TCM which gear position the shift lever is in (Park, Reverse, Neutral, Drive, Low).

faq.q: Can a misadjusted shift cable cause P0705?
faq.a: Yes. If the shift cable is stretched or misadjusted, the sensor position will not match the lever position, triggering this code.

faq.q: Is P0705 related to the neutral safety switch?
faq.a: Yes. On many vehicles, the transmission range sensor incorporates the neutral safety switch function.

title: Transmission Range Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: The transmission range sensor signal is erratic, out of range, or does not correlate with other inputs.
fullDescription: Code P0706 is set when the TCM/PCM detects that the transmission range sensor signal is intermittent, fluctuating between positions, or providing readings that do not correlate with other transmission data such as gear ratio. Unlike P0705 which indicates a circuit fault, P0706 indicates the sensor is working but providing unreliable data. This often points to a worn or contaminated sensor.
safetyImpact: Transmission may shift erratically. The vehicle could start in an unexpected gear position. Dashboard gear display may be incorrect.

causes.cause: Worn transmission range sensor with intermittent contact
Contamination or debris on the sensor
Loose shift linkage causing inconsistent readings
Intermittent wiring connection at the sensor

symptoms: Gear indicator on dashboard flickers or shows wrong gear

fixSteps.title: Monitor sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor the TRS signal in real time while moving the shift lever through all positions. Look for erratic readings, dropouts, or wrong positions.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and clean the sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the sensor and clean it. Check for wear marks, contamination, or damage. Clean the mating surface on the transmission.

fixSteps.title: Check linkage and adjustment
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the shift linkage has no play or slop. Adjust the TRS alignment so each position registers cleanly.

fixSteps.title: Replace the sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning does not resolve the issue, replace the TRS. Align carefully during installation and verify all positions read correctly.

faq.q: What is the difference between P0705 and P0706?
faq.a: P0705 indicates a circuit fault (open/short), while P0706 indicates the sensor is providing unreliable or out-of-range data even though the circuit is intact.

faq.q: Can a dirty sensor cause P0706?
faq.a: Yes. Many TRS use contact-based switches that can become contaminated with transmission fluid residue or debris, causing intermittent readings.

faq.q: Is P0706 urgent?
faq.a: It should be addressed soon as erratic gear position sensing can cause unexpected shifting and starting issues.

title: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The TCM/PCM has detected a malfunction in the transmission fluid temperature (TFT) sensor circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0710 is set when the TCM or PCM detects an abnormal reading from the transmission fluid temperature sensor. This sensor monitors the temperature of the automatic transmission fluid (ATF) and sends this data to the control module. The TCM uses this information to adjust shift timing, line pressure, and torque converter lockup strategy. A faulty sensor can cause the transmission to operate with incorrect parameters, potentially causing damage.
safetyImpact: Transmission may shift harshly or at incorrect times. If the fluid is actually overheating and the sensor is not reading correctly, transmission damage can result.

causes.cause: Open or shorted wiring in the TFT sensor circuit
Contaminated transmission fluid affecting sensor readings

symptoms: Transmission may not shift into overdrive
Transmission overheating (if sensor reads incorrectly cold)

fixSteps.title: Read sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read the TFT sensor value. It should start near ambient temperature on a cold engine and rise to 160-200F during normal operation.

fixSteps.title: Test sensor resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the TFT sensor and measure its resistance with a multimeter. Compare the reading to the manufacturer's temperature-resistance chart.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring and connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check the sensor connector for corrosion and the wiring for damage. Test continuity from the sensor connector to the TCM/PCM.

fixSteps.title: Replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the TFT sensor if it is out of specification. Some sensors are internal to the transmission (in the pan or valve body), while others are external.

faq.q: Where is the transmission fluid temperature sensor?
faq.a: It varies by vehicle. It may be in the transmission oil pan, in the valve body, on the transmission case, or integrated into the internal wiring harness.

faq.q: Can dirty transmission fluid cause P0710?
faq.a: Contaminated fluid can affect sensor readings over time, but P0710 usually indicates a sensor or circuit fault rather than just dirty fluid.

faq.q: What temperature should transmission fluid be?
faq.a: Normal operating temperature is 160-200F (71-93C). Above 240F (115C) is considered overheating and can damage the transmission.

title: Input/Turbine Speed Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The TCM/PCM has detected a malfunction in the transmission input speed sensor (also called the turbine speed sensor) circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0715 is set when the TCM or PCM does not receive a valid signal from the transmission input speed sensor. This sensor measures the rotational speed of the transmission input shaft or torque converter turbine. The TCM uses this data along with the output speed sensor to calculate gear ratio, control shift timing, and monitor torque converter performance. Without a reliable input speed signal, the transmission cannot shift correctly.
safetyImpact: Transmission may enter limp mode with harsh shifting. The vehicle may be stuck in one gear, making highway driving difficult or unsafe.

causes.cause: Contaminated or damaged sensor reluctor wheel/tone ring

symptoms: Transmission in limp mode (stuck in one gear)

fixSteps.title: Check transmission fluid
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the transmission fluid level and condition. Low or contaminated fluid can affect sensor operation.

fixSteps.title: Test the sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the input speed sensor on the transmission. Measure resistance across the sensor terminals and compare to specifications.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring and connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check the sensor connector for corrosion and the wiring for damage. Test continuity from the sensor to the TCM/PCM.

fixSteps.title: Replace the input speed sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is out of spec, replace it. The sensor is typically bolted to the outside of the transmission case. Check for metal debris on the sensor tip.

faq.q: What does the input speed sensor do?
faq.a: It measures how fast the transmission input shaft (connected to the engine via torque converter) is spinning. This helps the TCM calculate gear ratios and control shifts.

faq.q: Can I drive in limp mode?
faq.a: For short distances at low speed. Limp mode typically locks the transmission in 2nd or 3rd gear to prevent damage. Avoid highway driving.

faq.q: Where is the input speed sensor?
faq.a: Typically on the front or side of the transmission case, near where the torque converter housing meets the main case.

title: Output Speed Sensor Circuit
shortDescription: The TCM/PCM has detected a malfunction in the transmission output speed sensor circuit.
fullDescription: Code P0720 is set when the TCM or PCM does not receive a valid signal from the transmission output speed sensor (OSS). This sensor measures the rotational speed of the transmission output shaft, which directly corresponds to vehicle speed. The TCM uses this data for shift control, torque converter lockup, and speedometer operation. Without a reliable output speed signal, the transmission cannot function properly and the speedometer may not work.
safetyImpact: Speedometer may not function. Transmission shifting will be impaired. Vehicle may enter limp mode.

causes.cause: Damaged wiring or connector in the OSS circuit
Damaged reluctor ring on the output shaft
Metal debris on the sensor magnetic tip

symptoms: Speedometer not working or reading erratically

fixSteps.title: Check for related codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for all codes. ABS and speedometer-related codes may also be present, which helps confirm an OSS failure.

fixSteps.title: Test the output speed sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the OSS on the transmission (usually near the output shaft or tail housing). Measure resistance and compare to specifications.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring and connector
fixSteps.instruction: Check for corroded, damaged, or disconnected wiring. Test continuity from the sensor to the TCM/PCM.

fixSteps.title: Replace the output speed sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and replace the sensor. Clean the sensor bore of any metal debris. Install with a new O-ring to prevent fluid leaks.

faq.q: Is the output speed sensor the same as the vehicle speed sensor?
faq.a: On many vehicles, yes. The output speed sensor provides the vehicle speed signal to the TCM, PCM, and speedometer.

faq.q: Can metal debris cause P0720?
faq.a: Yes. Metal particles from normal transmission wear can collect on the magnetic sensor tip, interfering with its signal.

faq.q: Is replacing the output speed sensor difficult?
faq.a: Usually not. It is typically a bolt-on sensor on the outside of the transmission, accessible from under the vehicle.

title: Engine Speed Input Circuit
shortDescription: The TCM has detected a malfunction in the engine speed (RPM) input circuit it uses for transmission control.
fullDescription: Code P0725 is set when the TCM does not receive a valid engine RPM signal. The TCM needs engine speed data to properly control shift timing, torque converter lockup, and line pressure. This signal may come from the crankshaft position sensor, a dedicated engine speed signal wire from the PCM, or the ignition system. Without accurate engine speed data, the TCM cannot calculate gear ratios or determine appropriate shift points.
safetyImpact: Transmission shifting will be impaired. The vehicle may enter limp mode with limited gear selection.

causes.cause: Open or damaged signal wire between PCM and TCM
Corroded connector at the TCM or PCM
PCM not sending RPM signal to TCM

fixSteps.title: Check for related engine codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for PCM codes. If crankshaft position sensor codes are present, address those first as they provide the source RPM signal.

fixSteps.title: Test RPM signal at TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Using a wiring diagram, locate the RPM input pin at the TCM connector. Check for a signal with the engine running using a multimeter or oscilloscope.

fixSteps.title: Inspect wiring between PCM and TCM
fixSteps.instruction: Test the RPM signal wire for continuity between the PCM and TCM. Check for shorts, opens, and damaged connectors.

fixSteps.title: Repair wiring or replace sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any damaged wiring. If the crankshaft position sensor is faulty, replace it. Clear codes and verify proper transmission operation.

faq.q: How does the TCM get engine RPM data?
faq.a: Depending on the vehicle, the TCM may receive engine RPM from the crankshaft position sensor directly, through a shared data bus, or via a dedicated signal wire from the PCM.

faq.q: Can a crankshaft sensor cause P0725?
faq.a: Yes. If the crankshaft position sensor is faulty, both the PCM and TCM may lose the engine speed signal.

faq.q: Is P0725 a transmission problem?
faq.a: Not exactly. It is an input signal problem for the TCM. The transmission itself may be fine, but it cannot shift properly without engine speed data.

title: Incorrect Gear Ratio
shortDescription: The TCM has detected that the actual gear ratio does not match the commanded gear ratio.
fullDescription: Code P0730 is set when the TCM compares the input speed sensor and output speed sensor data and determines that the actual gear ratio does not match what was commanded. This means the transmission is slipping, not engaging the correct gear, or has an internal mechanical problem. P0730 is a general gear ratio code; manufacturer-specific codes (P0731-P0736) may indicate which specific gear is affected.
safetyImpact: Transmission slipping can cause loss of power, especially during acceleration or uphill driving. The vehicle may stall or be unable to maintain speed.

causes.cause: Worn transmission clutch packs or bands
Faulty input or output speed sensor giving false data

fixSteps.title: Check transmission fluid
fixSteps.instruction: Check ATF level and condition. Fluid should be red/pink and not smell burnt. Top off or replace if needed.

fixSteps.title: Scan for all transmission codes
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes from the TCM. Specific gear ratio codes (P0731-P0736) will help identify which gear is affected.

fixSteps.title: Test speed sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Compare input and output speed sensor readings using a scan tool. Verify they change appropriately during driving. Replace any faulty sensor.

fixSteps.title: Transmission service or overhaul
fixSteps.instruction: If fluid is good and sensors are working, the transmission likely has internal wear. A fluid and filter change may help in mild cases. Severe cases require overhaul or replacement.

faq.q: Does P0730 always mean a transmission rebuild?
faq.a: No. It can be caused by low fluid, bad sensors, or faulty solenoids. However, if the transmission is slipping due to worn clutches, a rebuild may be necessary.

faq.q: What does 'incorrect gear ratio' mean?
faq.a: It means the ratio between input speed and output speed does not match what the TCM commanded, indicating slipping or wrong gear engagement.

faq.q: Can a transmission fluid change fix P0730?
faq.a: Sometimes. If the fluid is low or degraded, a service can restore proper operation. If internal components are worn, a fluid change alone will not fix it.

title: Gear 1 Incorrect Ratio
shortDescription: The TCM has detected that the gear ratio in first gear does not match the expected value.
fullDescription: Code P0731 indicates the TCM has detected a gear ratio error specifically in first gear. When the transmission is commanded into first gear, the ratio between the input speed sensor and output speed sensor should match the known first gear ratio for that transmission. A deviation indicates slipping, improper engagement, or a mechanical failure affecting first gear components.
safetyImpact: First gear is critical for starting from a stop and low-speed maneuvering. Slipping in first gear can cause difficulty pulling away from stops and on hills.

causes.cause: Worn first gear clutch pack or band
Faulty speed sensor providing incorrect data

symptoms: Slipping when starting from a stop
RPM flare when starting from rest

fixSteps.title: Check fluid level and condition
fixSteps.instruction: Verify ATF level is correct and fluid is clean. Low or burnt fluid is a common cause of gear ratio errors.

fixSteps.title: Verify speed sensor data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to compare input and output speed sensor readings in first gear. Ensure sensors are reading accurately.

fixSteps.title: Perform transmission fluid service
fixSteps.instruction: Drain and refill the transmission fluid and replace the filter. In some cases, fresh fluid can restore clutch friction and proper gear engagement.

fixSteps.title: Professional diagnosis
fixSteps.instruction: If fluid service does not resolve the issue, the transmission needs internal inspection. A worn first gear clutch pack requires transmission overhaul.

faq.q: Can I drive with P0731?
faq.a: Limit driving. Slipping in first gear will worsen over time and can generate heat that damages other transmission components.

faq.q: Will a fluid change fix first gear slipping?
faq.a: It might help if the fluid is low or degraded. If the clutch material is severely worn, only an overhaul will fix the problem.

faq.q: How much does a transmission overhaul cost?
faq.a: Typically $1,500-$4,000 depending on the vehicle and transmission type.

title: Gear 2 Incorrect Ratio
shortDescription: The TCM has detected that the gear ratio in second gear does not match the expected value.
fullDescription: Code P0732 indicates the TCM has detected a gear ratio error in second gear. When the transmission shifts into second gear, the calculated ratio between input and output shaft speeds should match the known second gear ratio. A discrepancy indicates slipping, delayed engagement, or mechanical failure of second gear components.
safetyImpact: Second gear is frequently used during normal driving. Slipping can cause acceleration issues and overheating of the transmission.

causes.cause: Worn second gear clutch pack or servo
Faulty shift solenoid affecting second gear

symptoms: RPM flare when shifting to second gear
Transmission limp mode (often defaults to 2nd gear)

fixSteps.title: Check transmission fluid
fixSteps.instruction: Verify fluid level and condition. Low, dark, or burnt-smelling fluid should be addressed first.

fixSteps.title: Monitor live data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to watch input/output speed ratios during the 1-2 shift. Compare to expected gear ratios from service data.

fixSteps.title: Service transmission fluid and filter
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a complete fluid and filter change. Fresh fluid may restore clutch friction.

fixSteps.title: Internal transmission repair
fixSteps.instruction: If the issue persists, the second gear clutch pack or servo is likely worn. Transmission removal and overhaul is required.

faq.q: Why does limp mode use second gear?
faq.a: Second gear provides a balance between low-speed driveability and protection. It allows the vehicle to move without requiring the most vulnerable shift points.

faq.q: Can a solenoid cause P0732?
faq.a: Yes. A faulty shift solenoid can prevent proper hydraulic pressure to the second gear clutch, causing slipping.

faq.q: Is P0732 different from P0730?
faq.a: P0730 is a general incorrect gear ratio code. P0732 specifically identifies that the problem occurs in second gear.

title: Gear 3 Incorrect Ratio
shortDescription: The TCM has detected that the gear ratio in third gear does not match the expected value.
fullDescription: Code P0733 indicates the TCM has detected a gear ratio error in third gear. The calculated ratio between input shaft speed and output shaft speed during third gear operation does not match the expected value. This indicates slipping, delayed engagement, or mechanical failure of the third gear clutch assembly.
safetyImpact: Slipping in third gear affects mid-range acceleration and can cause the transmission to overheat during sustained driving.

causes.cause: Faulty shift solenoid for third gear
Valve body issue affecting third gear apply

symptoms: Lack of power during mid-range acceleration

fixSteps.title: Check fluid condition
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect transmission fluid level and quality. Address any fluid issues before further diagnosis.

fixSteps.title: Data log the shift
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to record input and output speed during the 2-3 shift. Compare the actual ratio to the expected third gear ratio.

fixSteps.title: Fluid and filter service
fixSteps.instruction: Change the transmission fluid and filter. This may improve clutch engagement if the fluid has degraded.

fixSteps.title: Transmission overhaul
fixSteps.instruction: If the code persists after fluid service, the third gear clutch pack needs replacement via transmission overhaul.

faq.q: Can a transmission additive fix P0733?
faq.a: Transmission additives may temporarily improve clutch friction, but they do not fix worn clutch material. They are not a long-term solution.

faq.q: Why does third gear slip?
faq.a: Third gear clutch packs wear over time, especially in vehicles that are driven hard, tow heavy loads, or have not had regular fluid changes.

faq.q: Should I get a rebuild or a new transmission?
faq.a: A rebuild is typically more cost-effective. A remanufactured unit is another option that offers a warranty. New transmissions are rarely necessary.

title: Gear 4 Incorrect Ratio
